channel.title: math on mahmood
channel.description: Recent content in math on mahmood

item.title: artificial intelligence homework 5
item.description: (20 pts) use the alpha-beta pruning algorithm to: compute and show the optimal path (explain). show the places you have pruned (explain) explain your process as we did in class! on the right are the values of the pair for a specific state that is associated with the node we’re at, which can be deduced by the min/max expressions on the left the step/state number is the rightmost one (in parentheses) the children of y2 get pruned at step 9 (all except 0, so w1,3,etc.

item.title: conv gan in pytorch
item.description: an convolutional generative adversarial network in pytorch, trained on the mnist dataset essential packages we need to import some essential packages: # <<src-activate-venv()>> import matplotlib.pyplot as plt import torch import torch.nn as nn import torch.nn.functional as F torch.manual_seed(0) # for reproducibility it’s common PyTorch practice to initialize a variable, usually named device, that will hold the device we’re training on (CPU or GPU). here, if the machine we’re using has a cuda-supported gpu available, we use that, otherwise we use the cpu, this variable stores our choice and is used later to pass data to the training device, if we’re training on the cpu, there would be no point in copying the data from the cpu memory to another block of cpu memory, but it would still work.

item.title: derivative
item.description: (JOHN H. HUBBARD, BARBARA BURKE HUBBARD, 2015) derivative in one dimension let be an open subset of , and let be a function. then is differentiable at with derivative if the limit another derivative notation to denote the th derivative of a function we write , this reads “the th derivative of with respect to ” alternative definition of the derivative in one dimension let be an open subset of and a function.

item.title: logic programming in common lisp
item.description: this document needs some polishing!, the backward chaining algorithm isnt finished for example logic programming in common lisp logical statements we make use of the symbolic capabilities of lisp, we use quoting and symbols to represent logical statements, e.g. given the following knowledge base of horn clauses: in lisp we could write it as: (defparameter *kb* '((implies p q) (implies (and l m) p) (implies (and b l) m) (implies (and a p) l) (implies (and a b s) l) A B)) *kb* ((IMPLIES P Q) (IMPLIES (AND L M) P) (IMPLIES (AND B L) M) (IMPLIES (AND A P) L) (IMPLIES (AND A B S) L) A B) the choice of the symbols implies, and could vary, but essentially we should write code that deals properly with these symbols based on their names.

item.title: artificial intelligence homework 4
item.description: all the code used here can be found at https://mahmoodsheikh36.github.io assume you have the following knowledge create a decision tree based on the knowledge base above (ignore direction). the graph with the electric layout algorithm: (defparameter *homework4-knowledge-base* '((implies (and a b ) e ) (implies (and a c ) k ) (implies (and a c d) f ) (implies (and c d ) g ) (implies (and e f ) h ) (implies (and f g ) i ) (implies (and f e g) j ) (implies (and k l ) (and m n)) (implies (and f d ) l ) a b c)) (graph-generate-tikz (generate-graph-from-knowledge-base *homework4-knowledge-base*) :directed t :node-distance 60) the same graph with the layered layout algorithm: (graph-generate-tikz (generate-graph-from-knowledge-base *homework4-knowledge-base*) :directed t :tikz-params "layered layout, grow=up, edges={-{Stealth[]}}, edges=rounded corners, level sep=40px") using backward chaining, try to prove using , and as the data.

item.title: artificial intelligence homework 3
item.description: scroll to the end if you’re only interested in the solution and the steps in raw text.. homework on constraint satisfaction problem using common lisp graphs graph image from homework: gotta solve this as an rgba map coloring problem make use of forward checking prioritize vertices with least constraining (least edges, least constraints) to most constraining (most edges), in case of a draw, use the variable with the most remaining colors (least constrained), if there is another tie, choose the vertex to the left first we define the graph: (defparameter *mygraph* (make-graph :adjacency-list '((a .

item.title: artificial intelligence homework 2
item.description: given the following game tree original tree image from homework: apply alpha-beta pruning, show the optimal tree and the pruned subtrees (explain) a step by step execution, at each step the modified node and the nodes that it depends on to determine its value are highlighted red, the assumption here is that the depth-first search algorithm prefers the left side (applies recursion to left node first, then right node), next to each node written are the values of for the state associated with it, the pruned nodes are colored blue.

item.title: cuda common lisp
item.description: using cl-cuda in common lisp from quicklisp (ql:quickload :cl-cuda) arch linux setup i had to install cuda for the nvcc compiler and more, and nvidia for the gpu driver at first you may have to use /opt/cuda/bin/nvcc until you reboot or extend your $PATH to check which nvidia driver is in use run: lspci -k | grep -A 2 -E "(VGA|3D)" if you have installed the nvidia package and your os still uses nouveau you need to reboot if it still doesnt use nvidia you need to update your while system with pacman -Syyu (you’ve been doing partial upgrades) essential concepts threads mostly from https://docs.

item.title: convolution
item.description: (Maria Vakalopoulou, Stergios Christodoulidis, Ninon Burgos, Olivier Colliot, Vincent Lepetit. Deep learning: basics and convolutional neural networks (CNN). Olivier Colliot. Machine Learning for Brain Disorders, Springer, In press. hal-03957224, 2023) convolution: the convolution operation is defined as the integral of the product of two functions after one is reflected about the y-axis and shifted: in essence, the convolution operation shows how one function affects the other discrete convolution: for two functions , the discrete convolution of and is given by: extending to 2 dimensional functions we’d get: in machine learning, specifically in convolutional neural network, the convolution operation is used do detect features in an image, one of the operands is the image and the second is the kernel, see 1 although the kernel has to be rotated around every axis before we begin sliding it over the image, otherwise we’d be doing cross-correlation instead of convolution here is an example of a flipped kernel: padding: if you look at 1, notice the edges essentially get “trimmed off”, converting a 7x7 matrix into a 5×5 one.

item.title: linearly mapping an interval onto another
item.description: basically the idea is that, given an real interval and the number such that and another interval , we wanna find the number such that we need to isolate : extending to higher dimensions assume a vector space with a dimension of , given the 2 sets of real intervals and a vector : where for all is bound in the interval , i.e. we wanna find the vector : such that so we write the linear transformation (might not be necessarily linear but affine) obviously this function doesnt preserve the origin as , so its an affine transformation, we separate the intercept so we can drop it and add it later: we drop the intercept and find the transformation matrix of the function without it, which is a square matrix of size im taking it for granted that the without the intercept is linear, in reality we need to check if the properties of a linear function are preserved in then we add the intercept with homogeneous coordinates to get the final matrix, which is a square matrix of size so to conclude, the formula transforms the vector from a given set of intervals onto another, resulting in

item.title: 2d transformations
item.description: we usually use a vector to represent a point in 2d space a general form of linear transformations (or more precisely, affine transformations) in can be wrriten as: or or, in matrix form, using homogeneous coordinates: matrices allow arbitrary linear transformations to be displayed in a consistent format, suitable for computation 2d translation given a point , we wanna reposition it with the translation distance this is an affine transformation, we deduce an affine transformation matrix: 2d rotation about the origin a rotation about the origin by angle can be done with the matrix: 2d scaling scaling is just multiplying the variables by some constants, it is defined using the cross product of the point by the scaling vector we deduce the transformation matrix: using homogeneous coordinates: linearly mapping a grid to another simplifying the formula from linearly mapping an interval onto another to 2 dimensions: mapping the point from the intervals , respectively, to the intervals , respectively

item.title: depth-first search
item.description: depth-first search is an algorithm for traversing graphs. dfs starts at an arbitrary vertex and progresses from neighbor to neighbor. a free vertex is one that hasnt yet been visited during the traversal, two timestamps are set for each vertex, discovery timestamp and departure timestamp, each timestamp is a number between 1 and . each such number corresponds to only one timestamp, the timestamps are stored in the following variables: discovery time: which is set when is first visited during the traversal departure time: which is set when is last departured next to each vertex is its discovery/departure timestamps Figure 1: an example of dfs execution

item.title: convolutional neural network
item.description: this is still very much a work in progress some of the math notation may be inconsistent, but things are valid and make sense when read in context (i should fix that though) citations (Peter Norvig, Stuart J. Russell, 2020) (Jianxin Wu, 2017) (Zhifei Zhang, 2016) and countless other resources that helped me form a good understanding of the topics related to conv networks motivation an image cannot be thought of as a simple vector of input pixel values, primarily because adjacency of pixels in an image matters.

item.title: maxima in lisp
item.description: i ended up giving up on using maxima as a visualization/symbolic math computation library for lisp, it wasnt made with this usecase in mind i guess, and so its an utter nightmare to lisp with it maxima is written in lisp and so it can be used as a modified/extended lisp runtime how to to start the lisp runtime use the command: rmaxima -r 'to_lisp();' can also run this in emacs’ slime/sly with (sly "rmaxima -r to_lisp();") alternatively, we can clone maxima’s source code to the subdirectory local-projects of the quicklisp installation directory (usually ~/quicklisp unless you’ve modified it), follow the instructions at https://sourceforge.

item.title: feedforward neural network
item.description: feedforward neural networks is a wider term than discussed here, whats discussed here really is a specific type we call a simple multilayer perceptron, but this serves as an introductory to other types of more complex networks a feedforward network has connections only in one direction. each node computes a function of its inputs and passes the result to its successors in the network. information flows through the network from the input nodes to the output nodes, and there are no loops.

item.title: decision tree
item.description: (Peter Norvig, Stuart J. Russell, 2020) a decision tree represents a function that takes as input a vector of attribute values and returns a “decision”–a single output value. the input and output values can be discrete or continuous for a wide variety of problems, the decision tree yields a nice, concise result. but some functions cannot be represented concisely, for example, the majority function, which returns true if and only if more than half of the inputs are true, requires an exponentially large decision tree.

item.title: graph
item.description: a graph is an ordered pair , where is a set whose elements are called vertices, and is a set of paired vertices, whose elements are called edges consider the graph usually a graph is stored in computer memory using an adjacency list, this is the assumption here consider the following algorithm which receives an undirected graph and returns a list of its edges the time complexity of this algorithm is the maximum number of edges in a simple undirected graph is in a complete graph, the rank of each vertex is , and it follows trivially that the sum of ranks is , and since each vertex touches 2 edges, the number of edges is let be a path in a graph if , then is a cycle a path is simple if no vertex appears in more than once a cycle is simple if no vertex except for the first one appears in more than once let be two paths in the graph , the concatenation of is the graph is connected if between every two vertices there’s a path a graph is acyclic if in there’s no cycles a graph is a tree if it is connected and acyclic

item.title: multivariate linear regression
item.description: (Peter Norvig, Stuart J. Russell, 2020) we can easily extend from univariate linear regression to multivariate linear regression, such that each example is an n-element vector. our hypothesis space is the set of functions of the form the term, the intercept, stands out as different from the others. we can fix that by inventing a dummy input attribute, , which is defined as always equal to 1. then is simply the dot product of the weights and the input vector, or equivalently the matrix product of the transpose of the weights and hte input vector: the best vector of weights, minimizes squared-error loss over the examples: here we have a continuous weight space and we cannot find a closed-form solution.

item.title: discrete math
item.description: set subset set A is a subset of a set B if all elements of A are also elements of B union intersection disjoint sets two sets are said to be disjoint sets if they have no elements in common multiset a multiset is a modification of the concept of a set that, unlike a set, allows for multiple instances for each of its elements. the number of instances given for each element is called the multiplicity of that element in the multiset ordered pair given are mathematical objects, is an ordered pair, , as an example, the coordinates on a function is an ordered pair cartesian product cartesian product of 2 sets is the set of all the possible ordered pairs that can be obtained by taking an element from as the first in the pair and an element from as the second assume the cartesian product of where is: assume power: or or assume or or need to prove: we split into cases: case 1: assume case 2: assume , same as case 3: assume assume need to prove: or or we assume in contradiction that and and exists an so that and or there exists an so that and we split into cases: case 1: there exists an so that and exists so that and there exists an so that and there exists so that and case 2: there exists so that and , a similar path to the previous case we arrived at a contradiction so the theorem is true relation assuming and are sets, if then is a relation from to binary relation a relation is a binary relation if over the field this may not be the proper definition, the one on wikipedia allows the codomain and domain to differ reflexive relation let be a relation over set we say is reflexive if for every its true that (or we can write ) isnt reflexive there exists so that matrices that represent reflexive relations are always in echelon form, e.

item.title: data structures course
item.description: for most data structures i havent implemented deletion yet.. also its good to note that most of the code isnt optimized to hell, but it doesnt really matter since its only for education purposes and you should probably be using the data structures provided by the programming language’s standard library data structure a data structure is a collection of data and operations on said data, the data is represented as objects, and each object x has: key(x): a key that is used to identify the object (usually a number that uniquely identifies the object) info(x) or value(x): contains the data needed to store for the object (usually without the key) for example, if x is a person, key(x) could be the person’s ID, value(x) would be information on said person, like age, height, weight etc.

item.title: linear algebra
item.description: this document needs some refactoring, some of the proofs may be incomplete i also need to divide this document into smaller notes that can be read independently of each other, since the order here is messed up anyway and it makes no sense to use a progressive layout field placeholder.. matrix a matrix is a rectangular array or table of numbers, symbols, or expressions, arranged in rows and columns, which is used to represent a mathematical object or a property of such an object a matrix can be constructed over a specific finite/infinite set of numbers for example which is the set of integers, when we say a matrix is over a specific field of numbers we mean that the numbers in the matrix are limited to that field of numbers consider this matrix over the field , where all the numbers are integers matrix multiplication let be matrices matrix multiplication is possible if and only if the matrices have the dimensions and the result matrix is defined by matrices can be multiplied by constants, let be a matrix and be a constant, the definition of such multiplication is: matrix addition matrix addition is defined as the addition of 2 matrices with the same dimensions elementary row operation augmented matrix for a given system of equation, an augmented matrix is the system of equations represented using a matrix square matrix a square matrix is a matrix whose width is the same as its height, i.

item.title: calculus
item.description: its important to read the exercises provided on every subject because i explain important things with exercises headlines without content act as placeholders for now. factoring formulas trigonometric formulas numbers natural denoted by , represents numbers used for counting, might contain 0 according to some definitions, integer denoted by , represents whole numbers (numbers without fractions), normal denoted by , represents all integers greater than 0, rational denoted by , represents all numbers that can be written as a fraction of 2 integers real denoted by , represents all numbers complex functions linear rational irrational exponential is an exponential function if the default exponent may be (euler’s number) log the logarithmic function is the inverse of the exponential function , meaning , defined for important logarithm formulas: natural log polynomial trigonometric cosine sine tangent function even function let be a real-valued function of a real variable.
